items.foco: Early vs late neonatal sepsis criteria
items.title: Early vs late neonatal sepsis
items.contentHtml:
Early vs late neonatal sepsis criteria

  • Early neonatal sepsis: occurs within the first 72 hours of life; The route of infection is vertical (transplacental or ascending from the birth canal). The most common pathogens are group B streptococcus and Escherichia coli.
  • Late neonatal sepsis: occurs after 72 hours of life; Its origin is nosocomial or community-based, with a predominance of coagulase-negative staphylococci and Staphylococcus aureus.
  • Gestational age and birth weight modify the clinical threshold: in extreme preterm (<28 weeks) the inflammatory response is attenuated, delaying signs.

items.title: Maternal risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Most important maternal risk factors

  • Clinical or histological chorioamnionitis: the most potent risk factor for early neonatal sepsis (relative risk >5).
  • Vaginal carriage of group B streptococcus without adequate intrapartum prophylaxis.
  • Prolonged premature rupture of membranes (>18 hours before delivery) and fever intrapartum maternal (>38 °C).
  • Others: bacteriuria due to group B streptococcus in current pregnancy, preterm delivery (<37 weeks), colonization by resistant Escherichia coli.

items.title: Neonatal SIRS
items.contentHtml:
Neonatal SIRS: diagnostic criteria

  • Neonatal systemic inflammatory response syndrome is defined by at least two of the following criteria, one of which must be temperature or leukocytes: body temperature >38 °C or <36 °C; tachycardia heart rate >180 beats per minute; tachypnea respiratory rate >60 breaths per minute; leukocytes >34,000/μL or <5,000/μL, or >10% of immature forms.
  • In neonates <7 days, the normal heart rate is higher; the 95th percentile is used to adjust.

items.foco: Blood culture as a gold standard diagnosis
items.title: Gold standard blood culture
items.contentHtml:
Blood culture as a gold standard diagnosis

  • The blood culture is the reference test to confirm bacteremia in neonatal sepsis; At least 1 mL of blood is required (ideally 2-3 mL) for adequate sensitivity.
  • Extraction must be performed before starting antibiotics, by peripheral venous puncture with strict aseptic technique (avoid contamination by skin flora).
  • Automated systems (BACTEC/ BacT/Alert) detect growth in 24-48 hours; They are incubated for 5 days before negativity is ruled out.

items.foco: Lumbar puncture indications in neonatal sepsis
items.title: Lumbar puncture indications
items.contentHtml:
Lumbar puncture indications in neonatal sepsis

  • The lumbar puncture is indicated in any neonate with suspicion of early neonatal sepsis if there are neurological signs (seizures, bulging fontanelle, irritability) or positive blood culture, and in any neonate with suspicion of late neonatal sepsis.
  • It should be performed before starting antibiotics, unless hemodynamic instability or risk of bleeding (platelets <50,000/μL).
  • In normal neonatal cerebrospinal fluid: leukocytes <30/μL (in preterm <20/μL), proteins <170 mg/dL, glucose >40% of plasma glucose.

items.foco: Initial empirical antibiotic: ampicillin plus gentamicin
items.title: Initial empirical antibiotic
items.contentHtml:
Initial empirical antibiotic: ampicillin plus gentamicin

  • The empirical regimen of choice for early neonatal sepsis is ampicillin (200-300 mg/kg/day intravenously divided every 8-12 hours) plus gentamicin (4-5 mg/kg/dose intravenously every 24-36 hours depending on gestational age).
  • This combination covers group B streptococcus, Listeria monocytogenes, enterococci and most enterobacteria.
  • Gentamicin requires monitoring of serum levels (trough <2 μg/mL, peak 6-10 μg/mL) and renal function.

items.foco: Alternative in suspected meningitis: ampicillin plus cefotaxime
items.title: Alternative for meningitis
items.contentHtml:
Alternative in suspected meningitis: ampicillin plus cefotaxime

  • If bacterial meningitis is suspected (due to symptoms, altered cerebrospinal fluid or positive blood culture), gentamicin is replaced by cefotaxime (200-300 mg/kg/day intravenously divided every 8-12 hours) to ensure good penetration meningeal.
  • The combination ampicillin plus cefotaxime covers group B streptococcus, Listeria monocytogenes, Neisseria meningitidis and pneumococcus.
  • Avoid ceftriaxone in neonates due to the risk of bilirubin displacement and kernicterus.

items.foco: Coverage for Listeria monocytogenes with ampicillin
items.title: Coverage for Listeria
items.contentHtml:
Coverage for Listeria monocytogenes with ampicillin

  • Listeria monocytogenes is a gram-positive intracellular bacillus that causes severe early neonatal sepsis and meningitis; ampicillin is the drug of choice due to its excellent cell penetration and bactericidal activity.
  • Gentamicin is added by synergy in vitro, but it is not recommended as monotherapy due to partial intrinsic resistance.
  • In those allergic to penicillin, the alternative is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or meropenem (not cephalosporins, ineffective against Listeria).

items.foco: Duration of antibiotic treatment according to culture
items.title: Duration of treatment
items.contentHtml:
Duration of antibiotic treatment according to culture

  • If the blood culture is negative and the neonate is clinically well, antibiotics are discontinued after 48-72 hours.
  • If the blood culture is positive without meningitis: 7-10 days of intravenous treatment for bacteremia due to group B streptococcus; 10-14 days for Escherichia coli or other enterobacteria.
  • If there is confirmed meningitis: 14-21 days for group B streptococcus; 21-28 days for gram-negative or Listeria monocytogenes.

items.foco: Use of meropenem in severe or resistant sepsis
items.title: Meropenem in severe sepsis
items.contentHtml:
Use of meropenem in severe or resistant sepsis

  • Meropenem (20-40 mg/kg/dose intravenously every 8 hours) is reserved for severe neonatal sepsis with organ failure, or when there is suspicion of microorganisms producing extended-spectrum beta-lactamases (ESBL) or resistant to third-generation cephalosporins.
  • It is also an alternative in meningitis due to Listeria in those allergic to penicillin, or in complicated intra-abdominal infections.
  • Its use should be restricted to avoid selection of resistance; It is not of the first empirical line.

items.foco: Intrapartum prophylaxis for group B streptococcus
items.title: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Intrapartum prophylaxis for group B streptococcus

  • intrapartum prophylaxis is administered to any pregnant woman with a positive vagino-rectal culture for group B streptococcus between 35-37 weeks, or with risk factors (rupture of membranes >18 hours, intrapartum fever, preterm delivery).
  • Regimen of choice: penicillin G sodium 5 million units intravenous initial dose, then 2.5 million every 4 hours until delivery; alternative: ampicillin 2 g intravenously initially, then 1 g every 4 hours.
  • In those allergic to penicillin without risk of anaphylaxis: cefazolin 2 g intravenously; with high risk: clindamycin (if sensitive) or vancomycin.

items.foco: Monitoring gentamicin levels and kidney function
items.title: Gentamicin monitoring
items.contentHtml:
Monitoring gentamicin levels and renal function

  • Gentamicin requires monitoring of serum concentrations to avoid ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity: trough level (<2 μg/mL) is measured just before the next dose; peak level (6-10 μg/mL) 30 minutes after infusion.
  • In neonates, renal function is evaluated with serum creatinine and urine output; the dosing interval is adjusted according to gestational and postnatal age (every 24 hours in >37 weeks, every 36 hours in 32-36 weeks, every 48 hours in <32 weeks).
  • If the trough is >2 μg/mL, the interval is prolonged; if it is >10 μg/mL, the dose is suspended and reassessed.
